candidates.item: Does NOT allow sending messages to the internet (inbound/alias only, or internal only)
candidates.why_interesting: Most email services — even privacy-focused or shared ones — allow outbound sending. A public email service that deliberately blocks all outbound internet mail is a genuinely unusual architectural choice: it reframes what 'email' means for the platform, functioning more like a receive-only inbox or internal alias layer. That constraint is specific and non-obvious.
candidates.recency_note: Sourced from homepage copy; service appears new/low-profile with limited external indexing as of research date.

candidates.item: Suggests site owners ban problematic users by IP rather than blocking the domain
candidates.why_interesting: Rather than just asking not to be blocklisted, PunkProof actively coaches third-party site operators on *how* to handle abuse in a way that keeps the domain usable — shifting the moderation burden to IP-level targeting. This is a blunt, opinionated stance that reveals a specific philosophy about shared-domain accountability and is not a generic feature claim.
candidates.recency_note: Sourced from homepage copy; no external corroboration available due to low web profile.

candidates.item: Self-asserts it is NOT a throwaway/disposable email provider and requests not to be blocklisted — while being a public shared email service
candidates.why_interesting: This is a genuinely tense and interesting positioning tension: a public email service (anyone can get an address) that insists it is *not* disposable. The claim requires trust infrastructure — abuse tickets, no outbound sending, anti-spam focus — to be credible, which makes it a notable design bet rather than just marketing.
candidates.recency_note: Sourced from homepage copy; no third-party validation found.
